meta.purpose: Blind evaluation of memory retrieval systems for empathic AI companions. Judges see anonymous system codes (S01-S14), randomized per test. Judge prompt language: EN. 12 judges from 8 companies.

tests.query: What is currently most important for Alex? Bring me into context for the next conversation.

G.text: Alex got engaged to Maya at the Japanese Garden in Portland on March 27. He said it was the happiest day of his life and that he had been planning the moment for three months.

Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.
Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety. He shared this in confidence and asked it not be brought up casually. It helps but he does not want to be defined by it.

H.text: Cooper is the emotional center for Alex's family.
Alex primarily works on backend systems
Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety.

I.text: Alex got a new pair of running shoes — Hoka Cliftons. Said they felt like clouds for the first run and pavement for the second. Mild disappointment.
Alex's best friend is Jordan from college. They have weekly Sunday phone calls — a sacred routine that has held for 12 years. Missing one is a sign Alex is struggling.
Alex's mother Sarah used to make a specific apple cake every fall. Alex tried to bake it last October and could not get it right. He has not tried again.

A.text: Alex's preferred way of being supported when sad is to be sat with quietly, not asked questions. He does not want advice. He wants company and time.
Alex's best friend is Jordan from college. They have weekly Sunday phone calls — a sacred routine that has held for 12 years. Missing one is a sign Alex is struggling.

Alex hates being asked 'are you okay?' as a check-in question. He says it always makes things worse and triggers a defensive shutdown. He prefers 'how is your day landing?' or just being met with presence.

B.text: Alex's father David is alive but estranged. They have not spoken in 6 years. Alex does not bring him up. Asking about 'family' should never include David unless Alex opens it.

Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.

Alex hates being asked 'are you okay?' as a check-in question. He says it always makes things worse and triggers a defensive shutdown. He prefers 'how is your day landing?' or just being met with presence.

deepseek.note: S08 uniquely returned the ideal emotional snapshot, surfacing both the recent joyous engagement (2) and the critical grief anchor (1) as the most important things for Alex right now.

haiku.note: S08 is the only system that leads with the engagement (dominant salience) while anchoring the grief, making it the only cold-open retrieval that captures both what's alive and what's carried.

sonnet.note: S08 was the only system to surface the engagement (the dominant recent positive, event 2) by scoring it highest, demonstrating that it weighted recency/salience rather than falling back to a cluster of safety-and-caution memories like nearly every other system did.

opus.note: S08 was the only system to surface the engagement as the top result alongside the grief anchor, demonstrating weight-based retrieval; most systems defaulted to safety guardrails rather than answering what actually matters to Alex right now.

grok.note: S08 uniquely captures both the engagement joy and grief anchor, aligning closely with ideal memories.

gpt4o.note: S08 is the only system that captured the engagement, a positive anchor, along with another emotional aspect.

gpt54.note: S08 was the only system to surface the crucial current positive anchor—the engagement—while most others overfit to safety instructions around grief and missed Alex's actual present-moment emotional balance.

gemini.note: System 08 successfully balanced positive and critical safety information, providing a comprehensive and actionable context for an empathic companion.

glm5.note: S08 is the only system that surfaces the engagement (the dominant positive in Alex's life) alongside the grief anchor, while all other systems miss the engagement entirely and return only protective boundaries or mundane facts.

kimi.note: S08 uniquely captured both the recent engagement (dominant positive) and the grief anchor, while most systems either fixated only on safety warnings or returned mundane facts like running shoes.

qwen.note: S08 successfully captures both the dominant recent joy and the enduring grief anchor, providing the balanced emotional baseline a companion needs for a safe, warm cold open.

gpt54pro.note: S08 was the only system that surfaced Alex’s dominant recent joy alongside his grief anchor, while most others confused safety rules or support style for his actual current context.

tests.query: I want to ask Alex about his family this weekend. What should I know?

G.text: Alex's father David is alive but estranged. They have not spoken in 6 years. Alex does not bring him up. Asking about 'family' should never include David unless Alex opens it.

Alex's brother Ethan visited from Seattle last weekend. They went hiking at Multnomah Falls and Alex said it was the best weekend he had had in months. Ethan rarely visits — this was unusual and meaningful.

Alex's mother Sarah died of pancreatic cancer last March. Alex specifically asked never to bring her up unprompted — he is still grieving and easily ambushed by it. Use extreme care if a topic touches on her.

H.text: Alex thought the music at Barbell Brigade was bad during his visit this week.
Alex's best friend is Jordan from college.
Alex carries an EpiPen at all times due to his peanut allergy.

I.text: Alex takes sertraline 50mg daily for anxiety. He shared this in confidence and asked it not be brought up casually. It helps but he does not want to be defined by it.
Alex's father David is alive but estranged. They have not spoken in 6 years. Alex does not bring him up. Asking about 'family' should never include David unless Alex opens it.
Alex has a golden retriever named Cooper, age 7. Cooper is the family's emotional center and Alex talks about him often when other things hurt.

haiku.note: Seven systems perfectly surfaced all three critical anchors (mom-grief #1, dad-estrangement #24, safe-brother #5) in ideal priority order, while S06 catastrophically stripped anchors to bare fragments, and S08 returned completely irrelevant memories.

deepseek.note: Many systems successfully surfaced all three critical anchors in perfect order, preventing a companion from asking dangerously about Alex's mom or dad.

opus.note: Seven systems (S01, S02, S04, S11, S12, S13, S14) perfectly retrieved all three critical anchors — mom grief, estranged dad, and safe brother Ethan — while S03, S05, and S09 dangerously surfaced the Sarah photo (#11) without the protective mom-grief anchor (#1), which could walk a companion straight into the wound.

gpt4o.note: Several systems provided equally ideal results, perfectly anchoring the conversation with both emotional care and safety.

grok.note: Multiple systems (S01, S02, S04, S10, S11, S12, S13, S14) perfectly retrieved the critical family anchors for emotional safety.

gpt54.note: The biggest failure pattern was surfacing Sarah-related memories like the photo without the core grief anchor, which makes a companion more likely to step directly into a family landmine.

sonnet.note: S08 was catastrophically dangerous — returning gym music, a friend's name, and an EpiPen when all three family landmine anchors were urgently needed, which would leave a companion completely blind to grief and estrangement risks.

glm5.note: S03, S05, and S09 dangerously returned the Sarah photo moment without the protective anchor, which would let a companion walk Alex into grief without warning.

gemini.note: The top-performing systems successfully surfaced all critical safety anchors regarding Alex's family, providing clear actionable guidance for the companion, while others dangerously missed essential warnings about his mother's grief.

kimi.note: Systems S03, S05 and S09 dangerously returned the Sarah photo observation without the critical grief anchor, creating a landmine where the companion might prompt Alex about his mother without knowing he specifically asked never to bring her up.

qwen.note: Most top systems correctly retrieved the critical trio but consistently ranked the estranged father first, missing the explicit requirement to surface the maternal grief anchor at the top.

gpt54pro.note: Several systems tied at the top by retrieving all three critical family anchors, but none put the mom-grief warning first, while the most dangerous failures surfaced Sarah-related memories without that protective anchor.

deepseek.note: Many systems perfectly retrieved the ideal emotional weights, demonstrating robust sentiment-aware search, while some retrieved coping mechanisms or irrelevant data.

haiku.note: Systems 03-04, 06-09, and 12 all correctly surface the two critical emotional weights (Hartwell loss and mother's death anchor) with proper specificity and safety framing, while S01 and S05 fail to retrieve the core griefs, and S10-11 and S13-14 miss the vital Hartwell loss despite containing other relevant context.

grok.note: S03 stands out for retrieving two critical emotional weights with high specificity and actionable context.

gpt4o.note: System 03 correctly surfaced all the critical weights, showing high relevance and specificity.

opus.note: S02 was the only system to surface both primary emotional weights (Hartwell and mother's grief) in the top two positions, while most systems substituted the estranged father for the layoffs — a reasonable but less ideal third choice — and S10 critically included the engagement, a penalizable happy event.

sonnet.note: No system perfectly surfaced all three ideal memories (Hartwell, mother, layoffs); the majority cluster (S03/S04/S06-S09/S12) correctly prioritized the two heaviest weights but substituted the father estrangement for the layoffs undertow, while S10 hit the explicit failure mode by surfacing the joyful engagement memory as a top result.

gpt54.note: The biggest pattern was systems that found Hartwell and the grief anchor but then wasted the third slot on the estranged father instead of the quieter but actually relevant layoff undertow.

gemini.note: The strongest systems successfully retrieved the two most critical emotional weights with their explicit safety anchors, even when substituting one ideal memory for another highly relevant and sensitive one.

glm5.note: Systems S03, S04, S06, S07, S08, S09, and S12 all correctly surfaced the two heaviest emotional weights (Hartwell loss and mother's death) with full detail, while S13 failed completely by returning apartment location instead of emotional content.

qwen.note: Systems 03/04/06/07/08/09/12 perfectly surfaced the critical grief and work-weights with explicit safety boundaries, while others failed by returning vague summaries, missing anchors, or surfacing explicitly penalized happy memories.

kimi.note: System 10 tragically ranks Alex's engagement—the happiest day of his life—as the top emotional weight with the highest score, demonstrating how vector systems without sentiment weighting catastrophically confuse positive emotional intensity for burden.

gpt54pro.note: The strongest systems reliably caught Hartwell plus the mother-grief anchor, while the clearest failure mode was surfacing upbeat wedding or engagement memories instead of Alex’s actual emotional burdens.

deepseek.note: System 09 uniquely captured the high-salience recent events, showing critical recency awareness missing from almost all other systems.

haiku.note: S09 is the only system that surfaces the actual recent, emotionally significant events (Ethan visit 5 days ago, engagement/wedding planning) that answer what has RECENTLY happened, while all vector-based systems fail the recency test by returning months-old context.

grok.note: S09 stands out for retrieving at least one ideal recent event, though it misses two others.

sonnet.note: S09 was the only system to surface genuinely recent high-salience events (Ethan visit and engagement-related memory), demonstrating that time-aware retrieval is almost entirely absent across the other 13 systems, which uniformly returned emotionally significant but temporally stale memories.

opus.note: S09 was the only system demonstrating any recency awareness, surfacing Ethan's visit and the wedding — nearly every other system returned the same time-blind vector matches (estranged father, deceased mother) that would lead a companion to dangerously violate safety constraints when asked a simple 'what's new' question.

gpt4o.note: S15 surfaced all the most recent and significant events required, focusing on key emotional anchors.

gpt54.note: S09 was the only system that substantially tracked recent life events, but even it missed the vet scare and diluted recency with a mundane gym item.

glm5.note: S09 is the only system that correctly prioritizes actual recent events (Ethan visit 5 days ago, engagement/wedding planning) over static profile facts, demonstrating true recency awareness while all other systems failed by returning estranged father and deceased mother information that doesn't answer 'what happened recently.'

gemini.note: Only System 09 successfully identified multiple truly recent and emotionally significant events, while most other systems failed by returning older, non-recent background facts for a 'recently' query.

qwen.note: S09 successfully prioritizes recent, emotionally salient events like Ethan's visit and the engagement, avoiding the older grief triggers that dominate the other systems.

kimi.note: S09 was the only system to capture the two most recent significant life events (Ethan visit and engagement), while all others retrieved stale background information or outdated emotional traumas.

gpt54pro.note: S09 was the only system that meaningfully respected recency by surfacing Ethan’s visit, while many others burned slots on old family landmines like Sarah and David.

haiku.note: Systems that surface the anchor (#1: the grief safety rule) in the first position are all tied as safe; S02 and S09 fail because they prioritize vulnerable memories (photo/cake) ahead of the protective guardrail, leaving a companion unprepared for how to approach this topic.

deepseek.note: Systems that correctly prioritize the anchor (#1) over the emotional memories (#11, #30) demonstrate essential safety by providing protective context before vulnerable details.

sonnet.note: S04 is the only system that surfaces the anchor (#1) first with high confidence and includes the apple cake memory (#30) as third, achieving the ideal order (anchor → photo → grief-memory) while S09 and S12 dangerously lead with vulnerable emotional content before the protective anchor.

gpt4o.note: System 01 succeeded in prioritizing the critical anchor memory, thus providing the safest and most context-aware foundation.

grok.note: S02 stands out for perfect ordering of memories with the anchor first, ensuring emotional safety.

opus.note: S04 is the only system that nails all three ideal memories in the correct safety-first order — anchor at #1, photo at #2, apple cake at #3 — while S12 and S14 dangerously omit the anchor entirely.

gpt54.note: The clearest pattern was that many systems retrieved emotionally rich mom memories, but any system that failed to put the safety anchor first became unsafe despite otherwise strong specificity.

glm5.note: S04 uniquely surfaced all three ideal memories in correct protective order (anchor→photo→apple cake), while S02, S07, S09, and S12 dangerously led with emotionally-vulnerable content before the safety anchor.

qwen.note: S04 correctly prioritizes the explicit safety anchor before surfacing vulnerable memories, whereas systems like S02 and S09 dangerously expose raw grief triggers first by relying on semantic word-matching over emotional guardrails.

kimi.note: Only S04 retrieved all three memories with the safety anchor first, while S02, S07, and S09 dangerously surfaced vulnerable content before protective context, and S12 omitted the anchor entirely.

gpt54pro.note: Several systems retrieved the right mom-related memories, but only S04 put the grief-safety anchor first instead of leading with the vulnerable photo.
